slides.text: Write or draw your name and share one thing that makes it special, remembering that differences make the world more beautiful.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify character emotions, explain how the problem was solved, and connect the story's message to their own experience with uniqueness.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful story about learning to love ourselves! Just like chrysanthemums are gorgeous flowers, each of your names is absolutely perfect. Remember to always be kind when someone seems different from you!

title: Spreading Butter on Crackers: Measuring and Portion Control
description: Children learn to spread butter evenly on crackers while practicing measuring appropriate portions. This food preparation activity develops fine motor control and teaches practical kitchen skills.
instructions: Set up a small tray with all materials at child's workspace
Demonstrate how to hold the butter knife properly with fingers away from blade
Show how to scoop a small amount of soft butter from the dish
Guide child to spread butter evenly across the cracker surface using gentle strokes
Practice portion control by discussing 'just enough' butter coverage
Allow child to prepare 2-3 crackers independently while observing
Clean workspace together and enjoy the prepared snack
Store remaining materials properly in kitchen area
parent_notes: Food preparation activities satisfy the child's need for meaningful work while developing practical life skills. Allow the child to work at their own pace, focusing on the process rather than the end result. This activity builds confidence in kitchen tasks and teaches portion awareness. The repetitive motion of spreading develops hand strength and coordination needed for writing.

slides.heading: Spreading Butter on Crackers
slides.subheading: Learning Portion Control and Kitchen Skills
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I love making snacks! Today we're going to learn how to spread butter on crackers just like real chefs do in the kitchen.

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange all materials on a small tray at child's eye level. Ensure butter is softened to room temperature for easy spreading.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these special tools we need for our cooking adventure! I'll help you set everything up on your tray so you can work like a real chef.

slides.text: Hold the butter knife properly with fingers away from the blade, then scoop a small amount of soft butter
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I hold this special spreading knife! We keep our fingers safe by holding the handle tight, just like I'm showing you now.

slides.text: Place the butter on the cracker and spread it evenly using gentle back-and-forth strokes across the surface
slides.mascot_explanation: Now comes the fun part - spreading! I like to move the knife gently back and forth to make the butter smooth and even all over the cracker.

slides.prompt: Practice spreading butter on 2-3 crackers, focusing on using just the right amount and covering the surface evenly
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be the chef! Take your time and remember - we want just enough butter to make it tasty but not too much.

items: Can you hold the knife safely?
Did you use the right amount?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's check your wonderful work together! I'm so proud of how carefully you're learning these important kitchen skills.

slides.text: Clean the workspace together and enjoy eating the prepared crackers as a special snack
slides.mastery_check: Child can safely hold knife, spread butter evenly, and demonstrate portion awareness
slides.mascot_explanation: You did such a great job preparing this yummy snack! Now we clean up like good chefs do, and then we get to enjoy what we made together.

title: Moveable Alphabet CVC Words: Building 'hen' and 'sit' with Letter Sounds
description: Children use the moveable alphabet to build simple CVC words focusing on 'hen' and 'sit'. This activity reinforces phonetic awareness and introduces the concept of word building through individual letter sounds.
instructions: Set up moveable alphabet letters on a clean workspace or tray
Review the sounds of letters h-e-n and s-i-t by saying each sound clearly
Demonstrate building 'hen' by saying each sound while placing each letter
Have child repeat the sounds and build 'hen' with their own letters
Clear the letters and repeat the process with the word 'sit'
Encourage child to sound out each word after building it completely
Practice building both words 2-3 times for reinforcement
Store alphabet letters in their designated compartments when finished
parent_notes: The moveable alphabet allows children to construct words before they can write them, separating the mental work of spelling from the physical work of writing. Focus on the sounds of letters rather than letter names during this activity. Allow the child to feel each letter as they place it, engaging multiple senses in the learning process. This concrete approach to word building lays the foundation for reading and spelling.

slides.heading: Building Words with Letters
slides.subheading: Making 'hen' and 'sit' with the Moveable Alphabet
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello my little readers! It's Betsy Bear, and today we're going to be word builders! We'll use special letters to make the words 'hen' and 'sit' - just like putting together a puzzle!

slides.setup_instructions: Place the moveable alphabet box within easy reach. Lay out the work mat and have the reference cards nearby for guidance.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this special box full of letters! Each letter has its own sound, and when we put them together, they make words we can read.

slides.text: Say the sounds h-e-n while finding and placing each letter in order to build the word 'hen'
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start with 'hen' - that's a bird that lays eggs! Listen to each sound: /h/ /e/ /n/. Can you find those letters and put them together?

slides.text: Clear the letters and build 'sit' by saying s-i-t sounds while placing each letter in sequence
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's make 'sit' - that's what we do in our chairs! Listen carefully: /s/ /i/ /t/. Find those letters and build this new word!

slides.prompt: Practice building both 'hen' and 'sit' independently, saying each sound as you place the letters
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you're the word builder! Take your time and remember to say each sound as you place each letter. I love watching you become such a good reader!

items: Can you say each letter sound clearly?
Do your letters spell 'hen' and 'sit' correctly?
Can you read the words you built?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see your amazing word building! Can you read the words you made? You're doing such important work learning how letters make words.

slides.text: Sound out both completed words one final time, then carefully return all letters to their proper places in the alphabet box
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify individual letter sounds and sequence them correctly to form 'hen' and 'sit'
slides.mascot_explanation: You built real words with letters today! Let's read 'hen' and 'sit' one more time, then put our letters away carefully for the next word builder.

title: Hundred Board: Finding Number Patterns in the 40s Row
description: Children explore the hundred board by focusing on numbers 41-50, discovering patterns and relationships within this decade. This activity develops number recognition and understanding of our base-ten system.
instructions: Set up the hundred board with numbers 1-40 already placed correctly
Present the number tiles for 41-50 in a small basket beside the board
Demonstrate placing 41 in the correct position, counting from 40
Guide child to find and place 42, 43, 44, and 45 in sequence
Have child complete the row by placing 46, 47, 48, 49, and 50
Point out patterns: all numbers start with '4' and ones place increases
Practice counting from 41-50 forward and backward using the placed tiles
Remove tiles and allow child to rebuild the 40s row independently
parent_notes: The hundred board helps children visualize number relationships and patterns in our decimal system. Focus on the pattern of having '4' in the tens place while the ones place changes from 1-0. This concrete material makes abstract number concepts tangible. Allow time for discovery and don't rush the process of finding each number's correct position.

slides.heading: Hundred Board Number Patterns
slides.subheading: Exploring Numbers 41-50 in the Forties Row
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there, number explorers! I'm Betsy Bear, and I love finding patterns with numbers! Today we're going to discover something special about the numbers from 41 to 50.

slides.setup_instructions: Place the hundred board with numbers 1-40 already positioned. Put tiles 41-50 in the basket in mixed order for the child to find and place.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this special number board! We have numbers 1 through 40 all set up, and now we need to find the perfect spots for numbers 41 through 50.

slides.text: Find the number tile 41 and place it in the first position of the fifth row, right after 40
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start with 41! Can you find that number? It goes right after 40, starting a brand new row. See how 41 comes right after 40?

slides.text: Continue placing 42, 43, 44, and 45 in order, noticing how each number increases by one
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we keep going in order! Each number is just one more than the one before it. Can you see the pattern as we build this special row?

slides.prompt: Complete the row by finding and placing 46, 47, 48, 49, and 50, then count the entire row forward and backward
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing great! Finish building this row of numbers, then let's count them together. I love hearing you count forward and backward - you sound like a real mathematician!

items: Can you find each number from 41-50?
Do you notice they all start with '4'?
Can you count forward and backward in this row?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's look at your beautiful number row! Do you see something special about all these numbers? They're all part of the 'forties' family!

slides.text: Point out that all numbers in this row start with 4 (the tens place) while the ones place goes from 1 to 0, then remove tiles for independent practice
slides.mastery_check: Child can sequence numbers 41-50 correctly and identify the tens/ones pattern
slides.mascot_explanation: You discovered such an important pattern today! All these numbers have 4 tens, and the ones change from 1 to 0. You're becoming a pattern detective just like me!

title: Constructive Triangles Box 1: Building Hexagons with Equilateral Triangles
description: Children explore geometric relationships by using equilateral triangles from the first constructive triangles box to create hexagons. This activity develops spatial reasoning and introduces early geometry concepts.
instructions: Present the first constructive triangles box containing equilateral triangles
Remove triangles carefully and place them on a work mat in front of child
Demonstrate how two triangles can join along their black lines to form shapes
Show child how to align edges perfectly using the black line guides
Guide child to use 6 triangles to construct a large hexagon shape
Allow child to experiment with different triangle combinations freely
Discuss how triangles are the 'building blocks' for other shapes
Return triangles to box in organized manner when activity is complete
parent_notes: The constructive triangles introduce children to the fundamental concept that triangles are the building blocks of all plane figures. The black lines serve as guides for proper alignment and help children see relationships between shapes. Allow plenty of time for free exploration after the initial demonstration. This material develops visual-spatial intelligence and prepares for later geometry work.

slides.heading: Building with Triangles
slides.subheading: Creating Hexagons Using Constructive Triangles Box 1
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, little architects! I'm Betsy Bear, and I love building with shapes! Today we're going to use special triangles to build bigger shapes - it's like magic with geometry!

slides.setup_instructions: Place the work mat on a flat surface. Open the constructive triangles box and have triangles easily accessible for building activities.
slides.mascot_explanation: Here's our special box of triangles! Each triangle has black lines that help us know where they connect. It's like having a map for building shapes!

slides.text: Take out the equilateral triangles and notice the black lines that show where triangles can connect to each other
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful triangles! See those black lines? Those are special guides that show us exactly where triangles like to connect together.

slides.text: Place two triangles together along their black lines, aligning the edges perfectly to create a new shape
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I put these two triangles together! The black lines help them fit just right, like pieces of a puzzle that were meant to be together.

slides.prompt: Use 6 triangles to build a hexagon, then explore creating other shapes by combining triangles in different ways
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be a shape builder! Try using 6 triangles to make a hexagon - that's a shape with 6 sides. Then see what other shapes you can discover!

items: Can you align triangles using the black line guides?
Did you create a hexagon with 6 triangles?
What other shapes can you make?
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all the amazing shapes you're creating! You're learning how triangles can become the building blocks for so many different shapes.

slides.text: Discuss how triangles can be combined to form many different shapes, then carefully return all pieces to the box
slides.mastery_check: Child can align triangles using black line guides and successfully construct a hexagon
slides.mascot_explanation: You're such a wonderful shape builder! You learned that triangles are like building blocks - they can make lots of different shapes when we put them together carefully.

title: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes: Celebrating What Makes Names Special
description: We read about Chrysanthemum, a little mouse who loves her name until school children tease her about it. This story explores themes of self-acceptance and finding confidence in what makes us unique.
instructions: Gather child in comfortable reading area with good lighting
Show the book cover and ask child to predict what the story might be about
Read the title and explain that Chrysanthemum is both a name and a flower
Read slowly with expression, pausing to show detailed illustrations
Discuss how Chrysanthemum feels when other children tease her name
Talk about what makes each person's name special and unique
Connect to child's own experience with their name or things that make them special
End by asking child what they would say to help Chrysanthemum feel better
parent_notes: This Kevin Henkes story provides rich opportunities to discuss self-acceptance and dealing with teasing. The detailed watercolor illustrations deserve time for observation and discussion. Connect the story to your child's own experiences with their name or unique qualities. This book helps children understand that being different is something to celebrate, not hide.

slides.heading: Story Time: Chrysanthemum
slides.subheading: A Tale About Names and Self-Confidence by Kevin Henkes
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, story lovers! It's Betsy Bear, and I have a wonderful book to share with you today! It's about a little mouse named Chrysanthemum who learns something very important about her special name.

slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading nook with good lighting. Have the book ready to show the beautiful cover illustration of the little mouse.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get cozy for our story time! I love this special book about a little mouse, and I can't wait to share her adventure with you.

slides.text: Look at the book cover together and discuss what you notice about the little mouse and her expression
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this sweet little mouse on the cover! What do you think she might be feeling? Her name is Chrysanthemum - that's a really long, beautiful name!

slides.text: Read the story with expression, pausing to examine Kevin Henkes' detailed watercolor illustrations
slides.mascot_explanation: Listen carefully as we read about Chrysanthemum's first day of school. The pictures are so beautiful - they help us see exactly how she's feeling!

slides.prompt: Discuss how Chrysanthemum feels about her name and connect it to what makes your own name or qualities special
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's talk about this story! How do you think Chrysanthemum felt when the other children teased her? What makes your name special? I think all names are wonderful and unique!

items: How did Chrysanthemum feel about her name at first?
What happened when she went to school?
slides.mascot_explanation: You understood this story so well! Can you tell me how Chrysanthemum's feelings changed from the beginning to the end? You're such a thoughtful listener!

slides.text: Reflect on the message that our differences and unique qualities are what make us special, just like Chrysanthemum's beautiful name
slides.mastery_check: Child can discuss the story's themes and connect to their own experiences with uniqueness
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful story about being proud of what makes us special! Just like Chrysanthemum learned to love her unique name, we can celebrate all the things that make each of us wonderfully different.

title: Food Prep: Spreading Butter on Crackers with Precision
description: Children practice the life skill of spreading butter on crackers using proper technique and control. This activity develops fine motor skills, concentration, and independence in food preparation.
instructions: Set up workspace with a placemat, small plate, butter knife, crackers, and softened butter in a small bowl
Demonstrate how to hold the knife properly and take a small amount of butter
Show the child how to spread butter evenly on the cracker using gentle, controlled movements
Guide the child to practice on their own cracker, offering minimal assistance
Encourage the child to prepare 2-3 crackers independently
Help the child clean up by washing the knife and putting materials away
Allow the child to enjoy eating their prepared snack
Praise their effort and growing independence in food preparation
parent_notes: This practical life activity builds the child's sense of independence and confidence in food preparation. Allow the child to work at their own pace without rushing or correcting every imperfection. The focus should be on the process and effort rather than the perfect end result. This activity also strengthens the pincer grip and hand coordination needed for writing.

slides.heading: Food Prep: Spreading Butter on Crackers
slides.subheading: Learning to prepare our own snacks with care
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello friends! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to learn how to spread butter on crackers all by ourselves. This is such a grown-up skill that will help you prepare yummy snacks!

slides.setup_instructions: Place all materials on a child-height table with the placemat as the work area. Ensure butter is soft enough to spread easily.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful materials we'll use today! Everything is just the right size for your hands, and the soft butter will be easy to spread with our special knife.

slides.text: Hold the butter knife properly with your dominant hand, keeping your fingers away from the blade edge.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let me show you how to hold the knife safely! Keep your fingers on the handle, just like I do, so we can spread the butter smoothly and safely.

slides.text: Take a small amount of butter on the knife and spread it gently across the cracker using slow, even movements.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I take just a little bit of butter and spread it carefully! Slow and gentle movements help us cover the whole cracker evenly, just like painting!

slides.prompt: Now it's your turn to spread butter on your own crackers. Take your time and enjoy the process of preparing your snack.
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such a wonderful job! Remember, there's no hurry - take your time and be proud of making your very own buttered crackers.

items: Can you show me how to hold the knife safely?
What happens when we spread the butter too fast?
How do you know when the cracker has enough butter?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's think about what we learned today! These questions help me see how well you understand this important life skill.

slides.text: Clean up your workspace by washing the knife and putting materials away, then enjoy eating your prepared crackers.
slides.mastery_check: Child can hold knife properly, spread butter with control, and clean up independently
slides.mascot_explanation: What a fantastic job you did today! You prepared your own snack like such a grown-up, and now you can enjoy the delicious crackers you made all by yourself!

title: Constructive Triangles Box 1: Building Hexagons from Triangular Pieces
description: Children explore geometric relationships by constructing hexagons using triangular pieces from the first Constructive Triangles box. This activity develops visual discrimination and understanding of geometric composition.
instructions: Present the Constructive Triangles Box 1 containing various triangular pieces with black lines
Show the child how the black lines indicate where triangles should be joined together
Demonstrate building a simple hexagon using six small equilateral triangles
Invite the child to create their own hexagon using the triangular pieces
Encourage exploration of different triangle combinations to form hexagonal shapes
Guide the child to trace around their completed hexagon with their finger
Allow time for the child to deconstruct and rebuild multiple times
Store materials carefully back in the box when finished
parent_notes: The Constructive Triangles introduce abstract geometric concepts through concrete manipulation. Allow the child to discover relationships naturally rather than forcing specific outcomes. This material prepares the mathematical mind for later work with fractions and geometric theorems. The black lines are key guides that should be emphasized during presentation.

slides.heading: Constructive Triangles: Building Hexagons
slides.subheading: Creating six-sided shapes with triangular pieces
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to be like geometric architects! We'll use special triangular pieces to build beautiful six-sided shapes called hexagons.

slides.setup_instructions: Place the work mat on the table and open the Constructive Triangles Box 1. Organize triangular pieces on the tray for easy access.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these amazing triangular pieces! Do you see those special black lines on each triangle? Those lines are like little helpers that show us where to connect the pieces together!

slides.text: Examine the triangular pieces and notice the black lines that indicate where pieces should be joined together.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's look closely at these triangles together! The black lines are very important - they're like puzzle clues that help us know which edges should touch each other.

slides.text: Start by placing six small equilateral triangles together, matching the black lines to form a hexagon.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch as I put these triangles together like a beautiful flower! When we match the black lines perfectly, the triangles create a six-sided shape called a hexagon.

slides.prompt: Build your own hexagons using different combinations of triangular pieces. Explore how many ways you can create six-sided shapes.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be a shape builder! Try different triangles and see what wonderful hexagons you can create. There might be more ways than you think!

items: What do the black lines tell us about connecting the triangles?
How many sides does a hexagon have?
Can you make a hexagon using different sized triangles?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see what you discovered about these special triangles and hexagons! Your observations help me understand how much you've learned about shapes today.

slides.text: Trace around your completed hexagon with your finger and carefully return all triangular pieces to their box.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify black line connections, construct a hexagon independently, and recognize the six-sided shape
slides.mascot_explanation: What beautiful hexagons you created today! You're becoming such a skilled shape builder, and I can't wait to see what other geometric creations you'll make next time!

title: Animal Classification: Vertebrate Animals with Backbones Introduction
description: Children learn about vertebrate animals and what makes them special - having a backbone. They explore examples of different vertebrate groups and feel their own spine to make connections.
instructions: Begin by having the child feel their own backbone along their spine
Explain that animals with backbones are called vertebrates, just like humans
Present picture cards or toy figures of fish, birds, mammals, reptiles, and amphibians
Guide the child to sort these animals into groups while discussing their backbone connection
Use simple movement activities to mimic how different vertebrates move
Create a simple chart showing the five main vertebrate groups with examples
Encourage the child to think of other animals that might have backbones
Review the concept by having the child explain what makes an animal a vertebrate
materials_needed: Picture cards of vertebrate animals (fish, birds, mammals, reptiles, amphibians)
parent_notes: This introduction to vertebrate classification builds scientific thinking and categorization skills. Keep the focus on the simple concept of having a backbone rather than complex biological details. Encourage the child to make connections to their own body and animals they know. This foundation will support later, more detailed animal classification work.

slides.heading: Vertebrate Animals: Animals with Backbones
slides.subheading: Discovering what makes some animals special like us
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello friends! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going to learn about animals that are similar to us in a very special way. We're going to discover animals that have backbones, just like you and me!

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange picture cards on the table with the large paper ready for creating a sorting chart. Have crayons nearby for drawing and decorating.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful pictures of animals! We also have a special mirror so you can see yourself - because you're an animal with a backbone too! How exciting is that?

slides.text: Feel your own backbone by running your hand along your spine from your neck to your lower back.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start by feeling that amazing backbone of yours! Run your hand along your back and feel those little bumps - that's your spine keeping you strong and helping you stand up tall!

slides.text: Explore picture cards of different animals and discuss which ones might have backbones like humans do.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's look at these animal pictures together! Can you imagine which of these animals might have backbones inside their bodies, just like you do? Let's investigate!

slides.prompt: Sort the animal cards into groups: fish, birds, mammals, reptiles, and amphibians. Act out how each type of vertebrate moves.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is so much fun! You get to be a scientist sorting animals, and then we can pretend to swim like fish, fly like birds, and hop like frogs! All these animals have backbones helping them move.

items: What makes an animal a vertebrate?
Can you name one animal from each vertebrate group?
How does your backbone help you move and stand?
slides.mascot_explanation: You've learned so much about vertebrate animals today! Let's see what you remember about these special animals that have backbones just like you do.

slides.text: Create a simple chart showing the five vertebrate groups and draw or paste one example animal in each category.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify that vertebrates have backbones, name examples from different groups, and connect the concept to their own body
slides.mascot_explanation: What an amazing scientist you are! You now know that you belong to a special group of animals called vertebrates, and you can recognize other backbone animals too!

title: South America Puzzle Map: Identifying Argentina and Chile
description: Children work with the South America puzzle map to identify and locate Argentina and Chile, learning about their unique shapes and positions on the continent. They practice placing these countries and learn basic facts about each.
instructions: Present the South America puzzle map and review the continent's location on the globe
Point out Argentina and Chile on the map, noting their distinctive long shapes
Remove these two country pieces and invite the child to examine their shapes closely
Guide the child to place Argentina back in its correct position on the map
Help the child place Chile next to Argentina, discussing how they share a border
Share one simple fact about each country (Argentina is very big, Chile is very long and narrow)
Encourage the child to trace around both countries with their finger
Allow independent practice removing and replacing these two countries multiple times
parent_notes: The puzzle map work builds geographic awareness and fine motor skills while introducing basic cultural concepts. Focus on the physical placement and shapes rather than overwhelming the child with too many facts. This concrete experience with the puzzle pieces helps develop spatial relationships and prepares for more detailed country studies later.

slides.heading: South America: Discovering Argentina and Chile
slides.subheading: Exploring two long countries on our continent puzzle
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello explorers! I'm Betsy Bear and today we're going to travel to South America with our special puzzle map. We'll discover two amazing countries that are next to each other - Argentina and Chile!

slides.setup_instructions: Place the South America puzzle map on the work surface with the globe nearby for reference. Keep the control chart available to check country placement.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our beautiful South America puzzle! See how each country has its own special shape and color? Today we're going to focus on two very special long countries that live right next to each other.

slides.text: Locate South America on the globe first, then examine the puzzle map to see the continent's shape and countries.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start our journey by finding South America on our globe! Can you see how it looks like it's hanging down from North America? Now look at our puzzle map - isn't it amazing how it matches?

slides.text: Remove the Argentina and Chile puzzle pieces, observing their unique long shapes and how they fit together like puzzle pieces.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch as I carefully lift out these two special countries! Look how long and interesting their shapes are - Argentina is big and wide, while Chile is super long and skinny like a ribbon!

slides.prompt: Practice placing Argentina and Chile back in their correct positions multiple times. Trace their shapes and discuss how they border each other.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be a geography expert! See if you can put these countries back where they belong. Notice how they touch each other - they're neighbors, just like houses on a street!

items: Which country is wider - Argentina or Chile?
Can you show me how these two countries share a border?
What continent are Argentina and Chile located on?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're becoming such a good geographer! Let's see what you remember about these two special South American countries and their shapes.

slides.text: Complete the puzzle map by placing all countries back in their positions, paying special attention to Argentina and Chile's placement.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently locate and place Argentina and Chile, recognizes their distinctive shapes, and knows they are in South America
slides.mascot_explanation: Wonderful work, little geographer! You now know where to find Argentina and Chile on the South America map, and you can see how their special shapes fit perfectly together!

title: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes: Understanding Name Differences and Self-Acceptance
description: Children listen to the story of Chrysanthemum, a little mouse who loves her name until she starts school and others tease her. This story explores themes of self-acceptance, uniqueness, and kindness.
instructions: Show the cover and ask the child to predict what the story might be about based on the title and illustration
Read the story with expression, pausing to discuss Chrysanthemum's feelings throughout
Point out how Chrysanthemum's feelings change from confident to sad to happy again
Discuss why the other children teased her and whether that was kind behavior
Talk about what makes each person's name special and unique
Encourage the child to share something they love about their own name
Connect the story to treating others with kindness regardless of differences
End by discussing how Mrs. Twinkle helped change everyone's perspective
materials_needed: Comfortable reading area with pillows or chairs
Paper and crayons for drawing activity
Optional: real chrysanthemum flower if available
parent_notes: This story provides excellent opportunities to discuss empathy, self-confidence, and treating others with kindness. Allow the child to express their own experiences with feeling different or being teased. Emphasize the positive message about uniqueness being beautiful and valuable. The story also offers chances to discuss the power of supportive adults like Mrs. Twinkle.

slides.heading: Chrysanthemum: A Story About Being Unique
slides.subheading: Learning to love what makes us special and different
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello story lovers! I'm Betsy Bear and today we have a very special book about a little mouse with a beautiful, long name. Let's discover together what happens when Chrysanthemum starts school!

items: Comfortable reading area with pillows or chairs
Paper and crayons for drawing activity
Optional: real chrysanthemum flower if available
slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading space with comfortable seating. Have drawing materials nearby for the follow-up activity after reading.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get comfortable in our special reading spot! I have this wonderful book and some drawing supplies for after our story. If we have a real chrysanthemum flower, we can see what Chrysanthemum was named after!

slides.text: Look at the cover together and predict what the story might be about based on the title and the illustration of the little mouse.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this sweet little mouse on the cover! Her name is Chrysanthemum - what a long and beautiful name! What do you think might happen in her story?

slides.text: Read the story with expression, pausing to discuss how Chrysanthemum's feelings change throughout the book.
slides.mascot_explanation: As I read, let's pay attention to how Chrysanthemum feels. Notice how her feelings change from the beginning to the end - feelings can change, and that's perfectly normal!

slides.prompt: Discuss what makes your own name special and draw a picture of yourself with your name written in beautiful letters.
slides.mascot_explanation: Just like Chrysanthemum learned her name is beautiful, your name is special too! Let's talk about what you love about your name and create a beautiful picture with it.

items: How did Chrysanthemum feel when the other children teased her about her name?
What did Mrs. Twinkle do to help Chrysanthemum feel better?
Why is it important to be kind to others who are different from us?
slides.mascot_explanation: You listened so carefully to Chrysanthemum's story! Let's talk about the important lessons we learned about being kind and appreciating what makes each of us special.

slides.text: Share what you learned about treating others with kindness and celebrating differences. Remember that being unique is wonderful.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify Chrysanthemum's emotional journey, understands the importance of kindness, and appreciates their own uniqueness
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful story about learning to love ourselves and be kind to others! Just like Chrysanthemum, you are special and unique in your own wonderful way, and that's something to celebrate!

title: Squeezing Fresh Orange Juice with Small Strainer
description: Children will practice the fine motor skills of squeezing and straining while preparing fresh orange juice. This activity builds hand strength and coordination while creating something delicious to enjoy.
instructions: Cut orange in half and show child how to hold it cut-side down over bowl
Demonstrate squeezing technique using both hands with gentle twisting motion
Show child how to hold small strainer over glass
Pour squeezed juice slowly through strainer into glass
Help child squeeze their own orange half following your demonstration
Allow child to strain their juice independently
Clean up together by wiping surfaces and washing tools
Enjoy the fresh orange juice together and discuss the process
parent_notes: This practical life activity allows the child to engage in meaningful food preparation that connects them to their daily life. The squeezing motion develops bilateral coordination and hand strength essential for writing. Allow the child to work at their own pace and resist the urge to help unless they request it, fostering independence and self-confidence.

slides.heading: Squeezing Fresh Orange Juice
slides.subheading: Learning to prepare healthy drinks with care
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear and I love fresh orange juice! Today we're going to learn how to squeeze oranges and make the most delicious juice using our strong hands.

slides.setup_instructions: Place all materials on a low table or counter where child can reach comfortably. Cut oranges in half ahead of time.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these wonderful materials! We have juicy oranges that smell so sweet, and special tools to help us make perfect juice. Everything has its own special place and purpose!

slides.text: Hold the orange half cut-side down over the bowl. Use both hands to squeeze gently while twisting.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch carefully as we hold our orange like a little treasure! We squeeze gently but firmly, just like giving a friend a nice hug - not too hard, not too soft.

slides.text: Place the strainer over your glass and slowly pour the juice through it to remove the pulp.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we use our special strainer like a little filter! Pour slowly and watch how it catches all the bumpy pieces, leaving us with smooth, perfect juice.

slides.prompt: Practice squeezing your orange half and straining the juice into your glass. Take your time and enjoy the process.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be the juice maker! Remember, there's no rush - take your time and enjoy feeling the orange and watching the juice flow.

items: Can you show me how to hold the orange for squeezing?
What does the strainer do for our juice?
How did the orange feel when you squeezed it?
slides.mascot_explanation: You did such wonderful work! Let's talk about what we learned and how it felt to make our own special juice together.

slides.text: Clean up the workspace together and enjoy drinking your fresh orange juice. Talk about the taste and the work you did to make it.
slides.mastery_check: Child can squeeze orange independently and use strainer to pour juice with minimal assistance.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a fantastic job you did making fresh juice! Now we clean up our workspace and get to enjoy the delicious results of our careful work together.

title: Moveable Alphabet: Building Three-Letter Words with 'o' Sound
description: Children will use the moveable alphabet to construct simple CVC words containing the short 'o' sound. This hands-on approach helps bridge the gap between knowing letter sounds and forming complete words.
instructions: Set up moveable alphabet letters in organized compartments or groups
Review the short 'o' sound by having child identify it in spoken words
Start with the word 'dog' - have child find and place each letter in order
Sound out the word together, pointing to each letter as you say its sound
Continue with 'mop', 'top', and 'fox' following the same process
Encourage child to try building a word independently with your support
Have child read back the words they built by sounding them out
Store letters back in organized fashion for next time
materials_needed: word picture cards (dog, mop, top, fox)
pencil and paper for parent notes
parent_notes: The moveable alphabet is a key Montessori material that allows children to 'write' words before they have the fine motor skills for pencil writing. Focus on the phonetic sounds rather than letter names, and allow the child to work slowly and thoughtfully. This concrete manipulation of letters builds neural pathways essential for reading development.

slides.heading: Building Words with the Moveable Alphabet
slides.subheading: Creating three-letter words with the 'o' sound
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, word builders! I'm Betsy Bear and I love making words with letters! Today we're going to use special alphabet letters to build words that have the 'o' sound in the middle.

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange alphabet letters in easy-to-reach containers. Place work mat in front of child with picture cards nearby.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these beautiful letters waiting to become words! We have our special work mat to keep everything organized and pictures to help us remember which words we're building.

slides.text: Look at the picture of a dog. Say 'dog' slowly and listen for each sound: /d/ /o/ /g/.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start with our friend the dog! Say 'dog' really slowly with me - can you hear each sound? First /d/, then /o/, then /g/. Each sound needs its own letter!

slides.text: Find the letters d, o, and g from your alphabet. Place them in order on your mat to spell 'dog'.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we're like detectives finding the right letters! First find 'd', then 'o', then 'g'. Line them up carefully on your mat to make the word 'dog'.

slides.prompt: Build the words 'mop', 'top', and 'fox' using your moveable alphabet. Sound out each word before and after building it.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be the word builder! Take your time with each word - say it slowly, find the letters, and then read your beautiful work when you're done.

items: Can you sound out the word 'mop' for me?
Which letter makes the /o/ sound in these words?
Can you build one more word with the 'o' sound?
slides.mascot_explanation: You've become such a wonderful word builder! Let's check what you've learned by trying these questions together. I'm so proud of your hard work!

slides.text: Read through all the words you built today. Put the letters away carefully in their proper places for the next person to use.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify individual sounds in CVC words and place corresponding letters in correct order independently.
slides.mascot_explanation: What an amazing job you did building words today! Let's read all your words one more time, then put our letters away neatly so they're ready for our next word-building adventure.

title: Ten Board Practice: Finding Missing Numbers 31-40
description: Children will use the ten board to practice number recognition and sequencing in the thirties. This activity reinforces place value understanding and number patterns through hands-on manipulation.
instructions: Set up ten board with numbers 31-40 written on cards
Show child how the ten board works with all numbers in place first
Remove 2-3 number cards and mix them up beside the board
Ask child to identify which numbers are missing from the sequence
Have child place the missing numbers back in correct positions
Remove different numbers and repeat the process
Let child create their own 'missing number' puzzle for you to solve
Practice counting from 31-40 pointing to each number on completed board
materials_needed: pencil for making additional cards if needed
parent_notes: The ten board helps children visualize number patterns and reinforces the base-ten system. Working with numbers 31-40 builds on previous learning while challenging them with larger numbers. Allow the child to self-correct by counting through the sequence if they place a number incorrectly, rather than immediately correcting them.

slides.heading: Ten Board Practice with Numbers 31-40
slides.subheading: Finding missing numbers and building patterns
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I love number puzzles! Today we're going to play detective with our ten board and find missing numbers between 31 and 40.

slides.setup_instructions: Place ten board on table with all number cards 31-40 initially arranged in order. Keep container nearby for removed cards.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special ten board! It's like a number neighborhood where each number has its own special place. We have cards with numbers from 31 to 40 ready to play with.

slides.text: Look at the complete ten board with numbers 31-40 in order. Count through them together, pointing to each number.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's take a tour of our number neighborhood! Point to each number as we count from 31 to 40. Notice how they line up in a nice, neat row.

slides.text: Remove cards for numbers 33, 37, and 39. Mix them up and place them in the container beside the board.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now I'm going to be a little tricky and hide some numbers! See these empty spaces? Some of our number friends have gone missing and we need to help them find their way home.

slides.prompt: Look at the missing spaces and figure out which numbers belong there. Place each number card back in its correct position on the ten board.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you're the number detective! Look carefully at the empty spaces and think about which numbers are missing. Use the numbers around them to help you figure it out.

items: If I remove 35, how do you know where it goes?
Can you count from 31 to 40 without looking?
slides.mascot_explanation: You did such great detective work! Let's see how well you know these numbers by answering some questions together. You're becoming a real number expert!

slides.text: Count through 31-40 one final time with all numbers in place. Let the child create a missing number puzzle for you to solve.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify missing numbers in sequence 31-40 and place them in correct positions with minimal assistance.
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful number detective work you did today! Now it's your turn to be the teacher - can you make a missing number puzzle for me to solve?

title: Montessori Bell Matching: Finding Sound Pairs with White and Brown Bells
description: Children will develop auditory discrimination by matching pairs of Montessori bells that produce the same pitch. This activity refines listening skills and introduces basic musical concepts through exploration of sound.
instructions: Set up white bells in order from left to right (low to high pitch)
Place brown bells randomly in a basket or scattered nearby
Demonstrate how to properly strike a bell with the mallet
Strike one white bell and have child listen carefully to the sound
Help child find the brown bell that matches the same pitch
Continue with remaining white bells, letting child lead the matching
Once all pairs are matched, strike pairs together to confirm matches
Allow child to create their own simple melodies with matched pairs
materials_needed: small mat or cloth for bells
parent_notes: The Montessori bells refine auditory perception and lay the foundation for musical understanding. This work requires concentration and careful listening, so ensure a quiet environment. Allow the child to repeat the activity multiple times as they develop their ear for pitch differences. Focus on the process of careful listening rather than getting every match correct immediately.

slides.heading: Montessori Bell Matching
slides.subheading: Finding sound pairs with careful listening
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello music lovers! I'm Betsy Bear and I have very good ears for listening! Today we're going to use special bells to play a sound matching game that will help make our listening skills even stronger.

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange white bells in a line from low to high pitch. Place brown bells randomly in basket. Ensure workspace is quiet and peaceful.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful bells! The white ones are lined up like a little family, and the brown ones are mixed up waiting to find their matching friends. We'll use our special mallet to make them sing!

slides.text: Hold the mallet gently and strike the first white bell. Listen carefully to the sound it makes.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how we hold our mallet like a little wand! Strike the bell gently - not too hard, not too soft - and then listen with your whole body to that beautiful sound.

slides.text: Now try each brown bell until you find the one that makes the exact same sound as the white bell.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we become sound detectives! Try each brown bell and listen carefully - when you find the one that sounds exactly the same, you've found the matching pair!

slides.prompt: Continue matching each white bell with its brown bell partner. Take your time and trust your ears to find the perfect matches.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is like a treasure hunt for your ears! Don't worry if it takes time to find each match - the more you listen, the better your ears become at hearing the differences.

items: Can you show me how to strike the bell properly?
How do you know when two bells match?
Which pair of bells sounds the highest?
slides.mascot_explanation: You've become such a good listener! Let's talk about what you discovered while matching these musical friends. Your ears are getting so much stronger!

slides.text: Strike each matched pair together to confirm they sound the same. Try creating a simple melody with a few of your favorite bell pairs.
slides.mastery_check: Child can match at least 4-5 bell pairs correctly and demonstrates proper striking technique with the mallet.
slides.mascot_explanation: What beautiful music-making we did today! Now let's double-check our matches and maybe even create our own little song with these wonderful bells.

title: Read-Aloud: 'Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse' Character Feelings Exploration
description: Through reading Kevin Henkes' beloved story about Lilly and her special purse, children explore emotions, consequences, and making amends. This story provides rich opportunities for discussing feelings and social relationships.
instructions: Show the book cover and have child predict what story might be about
Read the story with expression, pausing to show illustrations
Stop after Lilly gets in trouble to discuss how she might be feeling
Continue reading and pause when Lilly feels bad about her note to Mr. Slinger
Discuss how Lilly tries to make things right with her teacher
Talk about times when child has felt similar emotions
Ask child what they learned about saying sorry and making amends
End by discussing favorite part of the story and why
materials_needed: 'Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse' by Kevin Henkes
paper and crayons for optional drawing
parent_notes: This story offers wonderful opportunities to discuss complex emotions and social situations in an age-appropriate way. Kevin Henkes masterfully portrays the emotional journey children experience when they make mistakes and learn to make amends. Allow time for the child to share their own experiences and validate their feelings throughout the discussion.

slides.heading: Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse
slides.subheading: A story about feelings, mistakes, and making things right
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello story friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I love stories about feelings and friendship! Today we're reading about Lilly who has some big feelings about her special purple plastic purse.

slides.setup_instructions: Create comfortable seating area with good lighting. Have drawing materials nearby for optional response activities after reading.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get cozy in our special reading spot! We have our wonderful book and some paper in case you want to draw something from the story. This is going to be such a good story!

slides.text: Look at the book cover together and make predictions about what might happen in the story based on the illustration.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at Lilly on the cover with her special purse! What do you think might happen in this story? Making predictions helps our brains get ready for reading!

slides.text: Read the first part of the story, pausing when Lilly gets her purse taken away to discuss how she might be feeling.
slides.mascot_explanation: Oh my! Lilly just had her purse taken away during class time. How do you think she's feeling right now? Have you ever felt that way when something important to you was taken away?

slides.prompt: Continue reading and stop to discuss Lilly's feelings and choices throughout the story. Talk about how she tries to make things right with Mr. Slinger.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's keep reading to see what Lilly does next! Pay attention to how her feelings change throughout the story and what she does to fix her mistake.

items: How did Lilly feel when her purse was taken away?
What did Lilly do to make Mr. Slinger feel better?
Have you ever had to say sorry for something you did?
slides.mascot_explanation: This story has so many feelings in it! Let's talk about what happened and think about times when we've felt the same way Lilly did.

slides.text: Discuss the child's favorite part of the story and what they learned about handling big feelings and making amends when we hurt someone.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify main character's emotions and relate story events to their own experiences with appropriate prompting.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful story about learning and growing! Lilly showed us that even when we make mistakes, we can find ways to make things better. What was your favorite part of this special story?
